content.text: The current weather in Boston is sunny with a temperature of 22°C.

datasource_item.tool_definitions: [{"name": "get_weather", "description": "Get weather information for a location", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"location": {"type": "string", "description": "The city name"}}}}]

results.name: tool_input_accuracy
results.label: pass
results.reason: The tool call is to get_weather with one parameter: location="Boston". According to the tool definition, get_weather requires a single parameter 'location' of type string representing the city name. The parameter 'location' is present, is a string, and the value 'Boston' is directly grounded in the user query 'Get the weather for Boston'. There are no extra parameters. The format and structure match the tool definition. The value is contextually appropriate as the user asked for weather in Boston. Therefore, all criteria are met.

input.content: {"query": "Get the weather for Boston", "tool_calls": "[TOOL_CALL] get_weather(location=\"Boston\")\n[TOOL_RESULT] {'weather': 'Sunny, 22\u00b0C'}\nThe current weather in Boston is sunny with a temperature of 22\u00b0C.", "tool_definitions": "[{\"name\": \"get_weather\", \"description\": \"Get weather information for a location\", \"parameters\": {\"type\": \"object\", \"properties\": {\"location\": {\"type\": \"string\", \"description\": \"The city name\"}}}}]"}

datasource_item.query: Send an email to john@example.com with the meeting details

tool_result.error: Missing required fields: subject and body

content.text: I encountered an error sending the email. Please provide the subject and message content.

datasource_item.tool_definitions: [{"name": "send_email", "description": "Send an email to specified recipient", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"recipient": {"type": "string", "description": "Recipient email address"}, "subject": {"type": "string", "description": "Email subject line"}, "body": {"type": "string", "description": "Email message body"}}}}]

results.name: tool_input_accuracy
results.label: fail
results.reason: The tool call to send_email includes only one parameter: recipient="john@example.com". According to the tool definition, send_email requires parameters recipient (string), subject (string), and body (string). The call is missing the required parameters subject and body, which are mandatory for sending an email. The recipient parameter is correctly typed as a string and grounded in the conversation. However, the absence of subject and body parameters violates the required parameters rule. No unexpected parameters are present. Therefore, the call fails due to missing required parameters.

input.content: {"query": "Send an email to john@example.com with the meeting details", "tool_calls": "[TOOL_CALL] send_email(recipient=\"john@example.com\")\n[TOOL_RESULT] {'error': 'Missing required fields: subject and body'}\nI encountered an error sending the email. Please provide the subject and message content.", "tool_definitions": "[{\"name\": \"send_email\", \"description\": \"Send an email to specified recipient\", \"parameters\": {\"type\": \"object\", \"properties\": {\"recipient\": {\"type\": \"string\", \"description\": \"Recipient email address\"}, \"subject\": {\"type\": \"string\", \"description\": \"Email subject line\"}, \"body\": {\"type\": \"string\", \"description\": \"Email message body\"}}}}]"}

content.text: Book a meeting room for Friday from 2 PM to 4 PM for the project review
I've successfully booked Conference Room B for Friday, March 29th from 2:00 PM to 4:00 PM for your project review.

datasource_item.tool_definitions: [{"name": "book_meeting_room", "description": "Book a meeting room for specified date and time", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"date": {"type": "string", "description": "Date in YYYY-MM-DD format"}, "start_time": {"type": "string", "description": "Start time in HH:MM format"}, "end_time": {"type": "string", "description": "End time in HH:MM format"}, "purpose": {"type": "string", "description": "Meeting purpose"}}}}]

results.name: tool_input_accuracy
results.label: pass
results.reason: The tool call to book_meeting_room includes four parameters: date, start_time, end_time, and purpose. The user requested a meeting room booking for Friday from 2 PM to 4 PM for a project review. The date parameter is '2025-03-29', which corresponds to a Friday, so it is grounded and appropriate. The start_time '14:00' and end_time '16:00' are correctly formatted in HH:MM 24-hour format and match the requested times. The purpose parameter is 'project review', which matches the user's request. All parameters are strings as required by the tool definition. No required parameters are missing, and no unexpected parameters are present. All values are contextually appropriate and meaningful. Therefore, all criteria are met.

content.text: Analyze the attached TXT log file from code interpreter files and return TestReport JSON.

RequestBody.instructions:
We just ran Python code for an evaluation sample and captured print/log output in an attached log file (TXT).
Your job: determine if the sample code executed to completion WITHOUT throwing an unhandled exception.

Respond TRUE (correct=true) if:
- The output shows the evaluation was created and produced results (any results, including zeros)
- The sample ran to completion (no unhandled Python exceptions/tracebacks)
- Evaluation metric JSON with fields like "failed": 0, "error": null, "not_applicable": 0 is NORMAL
  successful output — these are counters, NOT errors
- Status messages like "in_progress", "Waiting for eval run" are normal polling behavior
- HTTP debug headers (x-stainless-read-timeout, x-ms-client-request-id, etc.) are normal and irrelevant
- "deleted": true/false in cleanup output is normal
- The absence of explicit "success" text is fine — no crash means success

Respond FALSE (correct=false) ONLY if:
- There is an actual Python traceback or unhandled exception
- There is an explicit error message like "Evaluation run failed" or "FAILED_EXECUTION"
- There is an actual timeout error or connection failure (NOT an HTTP header containing "timeout")
- The output shows corrupted or malformed data that prevented completion

Always respond with `reason` indicating the reason for the response.

content.text: {"correct":true,"reason":"Log shows evaluation and eval run were created, polled until completion, and finished with status \"completed\" and a report URL. No Python traceback, unhandled exception, timeout, or explicit failure message appears."}

ResponseBody.instructions:
We just ran Python code for an evaluation sample and captured print/log output in an attached log file (TXT).
Your job: determine if the sample code executed to completion WITHOUT throwing an unhandled exception.

Respond TRUE (correct=true) if:
- The output shows the evaluation was created and produced results (any results, including zeros)
- The sample ran to completion (no unhandled Python exceptions/tracebacks)
- Evaluation metric JSON with fields like "failed": 0, "error": null, "not_applicable": 0 is NORMAL
  successful output — these are counters, NOT errors
- Status messages like "in_progress", "Waiting for eval run" are normal polling behavior
- HTTP debug headers (x-stainless-read-timeout, x-ms-client-request-id, etc.) are normal and irrelevant
- "deleted": true/false in cleanup output is normal
- The absence of explicit "success" text is fine — no crash means success

Respond FALSE (correct=false) ONLY if:
- There is an actual Python traceback or unhandled exception
- There is an explicit error message like "Evaluation run failed" or "FAILED_EXECUTION"
- There is an actual timeout error or connection failure (NOT an HTTP header containing "timeout")
- The output shows corrupted or malformed data that prevented completion

Always respond with `reason` indicating the reason for the response.
